blocks.content: The morning was overcast, which suited Ethan just fine.
He had been at The Copper Mug for twenty minutes when Mr. Alvarez set a second coffee in front of him without being asked. Ethan looked up.
Mr. Alvarez agreed, and didn't move.

He pulled out the chair across from him — the one Cassandra had sat in three days ago — and settled into it with the unhurried ease of someone who owned both the chair and however much time was required.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You are looking at the table. It is not a very interesting table.
Ethan glanced at his sketchbook, still closed beside his elbow. He'd meant to open it. He hadn't yet.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Yes. That is a different thing from what I said.

He picked up his own cup from the counter as he passed it, sat down properly. The morning moved around them — a man at the door with a to-go order, the grind of beans from the back, the flat grey light coming through the window.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You have been coming here for almost three years.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: In two and a half years, you have drawn at this table on every visit. Except for the last five days.
Mr. Alvarez nodded with the look of someone for whom this confirmed a theory they had been quietly developing.
He looked at the window. Outside, the first slow drops of rain were beginning to darken the pavement.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: She makes it easy to be—
He stopped. Searched for the word.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Present. She doesn't ask me to be anyone I'm not.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: She makes the room feel different.
Mr. Alvarez looked at him with that particular expression of his — not pity, not sentimentality, just recognition. The expression of someone who has seen a thing before and knows what it is.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: That is not a small thing. For a man who has been comfortable in empty rooms.
